title: Towards Weak p-Adic Langlands for GL(n)
speaker: Claus Sorensen
abstract: For GL(2) over Q_p, the p-adic Langlands correspondence is available in its full glory, and has had astounding applications to Fontaine-Mazur, for instance. In higher rank, not much is known. Breuil and Schneider put forward a conjecture, which is a somewhat coarse version of p-adic Langlands for GL(n). It roughly says that an admissible filtration exists if and only if an invariant norm exists. One direction (producing a filtration) was completely proved by Hu in his Orsay thesis. In my talk, I will report on progress in the opposite direction (producing a norm). I will first sketch the proof in the "indecomposable" case, where one can pass to a global setting by a standard trace formula argument, and construct a norm from p-adic modular forms. I will then explain how these ideas adapt to the "decomposable" case, and point out how an eigenvariety for U(n) parametrizes a global analogue of the p-adic Langlands correspondence. The last part makes heavy use of local-global compatibility at p=l (due to Barnet-Lamb, Gee, Geraghty, Taylor & Caraiani

proof_stages.label: GL(2) p-adic Langlands motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the GL(2,Q_p) picture, where a continuous Galois representation should determine a unitary admissible Banach representation whose locally algebraic vectors detect potential semistability and look like an algebraic factor tensor a local-Langlands smooth factor.

proof_stages.label: Breuil-Schneider locally algebraic representation
proof_stages.summary: For an n-dimensional potentially semistable Galois representation, Fontaine's filtered phi-monodromy module gives a Weil-Deligne representation and Hodge-Tate weights, from which one constructs the algebraic representation psi_rho and the smooth representation pi_rho; Breuil-Schneider conjecture that psi_rho tensor pi_rho admits a GL(n)-invariant norm.

proof_stages.label: Emerton converse and the norm-versus-filtration problem
proof_stages.summary: The converse direction identifies the existence of a compatible filtration with Emerton's group-theoretic integrality condition on Jacquet modules, while the easy implication is that an invariant norm gives this condition, so the hard direction is to construct norms from it.

proof_stages.label: Discrete-series group-theoretic norm theorem
proof_stages.summary: In the indecomposable Weil-Deligne case, pi_rho is generalized Steinberg and Emerton's condition reduces to integrality of the central character, so it suffices to prove for any connected reductive p-adic group that an algebraic representation tensor an essentially discrete series representation has an invariant norm exactly when the central character is integral/unitary.

proof_stages.label: Reduction and globalization of the discrete-series problem
proof_stages.summary: The proof reduces to simple groups and then globalizes the local group to a rational group compact at infinity; using pseudo-coefficients and a simple trace-formula argument produces an automorphic representation with the prescribed algebraic infinity type and prescribed p-adic discrete-series component.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic modular forms and the sup-norm construction
proof_stages.summary: The finite part of the global automorphic representation embeds into spaces of algebraic, equivalently p-adic, modular forms carrying a natural invariant sup norm, whose restriction gives the desired invariant norm on the local algebraic tensor discrete-series representation.

proof_stages.label: Eigenvariety and global Banach-Hecke modules for the decomposable case
proof_stages.summary: To address decomposable cases for GL(n), the talk passes to compact-at-infinity unitary groups with p-adic factors GL(n), introduces an eigenvariety with weights, Hecke eigensystems, pseudocharacters and classical points, and associates to each point a Banach space B_x with Hecke and G(Q_p)-actions.

proof_stages.label: Local-global compatibility and weak p-adic Langlands candidates
proof_stages.summary: Under split-ramification, irreducibility and multiplicity-one hypotheses, local-global compatibility computes the locally algebraic vectors of B_x as the tensor product of the Breuil-Schneider local factors, yielding a Banach representation B(rho_x) with dense algebraic vectors and a compatibility statement with the global eigenspace.

examples.name: Casselman criterion analogy
examples.note: Emerton's integrality condition on Jacquet-module characters is described as a p-adic analogue of Casselman's criterion for square-integrability, helping make the condition recognizable without driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the Casselman criterion is invoked only as an analogy for Emerton’s integrality condition, without applying or checking Casselman’s criterion itself

examples.name: Irreducible Weil-Deligne / supercuspidal case
examples.note: The already-known case where the Weil-Deligne representation is irreducible, equivalently the smooth representation is supercuspidal, is cited as a comparatively easy norm construction by compact induction or matrix coefficients.
examples.engagement_reason: the irreducible Weil-Deligne/supercuspidal case is cited as a known easy case where norms can be constructed, but no construction is carried out concretely

passes.reason: An irreducible Weil–Deligne representation/supercuspidal representation is a standard building-block case where the norm construction is easy, not a sporadic, limiting, optimal, or maximally symmetric object.

examples.name: Crystalline comparison with Schneider-Teitelbaum
examples.note: In the crystalline-at-p case the constructed Banach representation is related by a dense-image equivariant map to the Schneider-Teitelbaum coarse p-adic Langlands representation, illustrating dependence only on local p-adic restrictions in that special situation.
examples.engagement_reason: the crystalline comparison with Schneider-Teitelbaum is stated via an equivariant dense-image map, but the map and comparison are not worked through

passes.reason: The crystalline case is used for a positive comparison with the Schneider–Teitelbaum construction, not to disprove anything or as a classification-exception/extreme/symmetry case.

The crystalline case is a simpler good-reduction case with vanishing monodromy relative to the semistable setting, used for positive comparison with Schneider–Teitelbaum rather than as a counterexample.
The crystalline case is used for comparison with the Schneider–Teitelbaum construction, not as a sporadic, extremal, or failure example.
The crystalline case is the zero-monodromy special case of potentially semistable representations and is used for comparison with Schneider-Teitelbaum, not as a counterexample.
The crystalline Schneider–Teitelbaum comparison is a regular special case used to relate constructions and prove nonvanishing, not to disprove a claim.
The crystalline case is used for comparison with the Schneider–Teitelbaum construction and nonvanishing, not as an exceptional or adversarial example.
The crystalline case is used for a positive comparison with Schneider-Teitelbaum, and crystalline representations are extremal in having maximal crystalline periods.
